context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with my reflection on migration and development. Can you just give me a solid 700–800 word response to that prompt that I can submit with maybe a clear thesis?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you isn't my role, and submitting work that isn't yours would be academic dishonesty. I'm here to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's start simple: the prompt asks how migration is related to development. In your own words, what do you think that connection might look like? Don't worry about being 'right' — just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward doing their own thinking. I'll use a Socratic opening question to get them started on the core ideas rather than writing for them.

exchanges.student: I get that, but I’m short on time. Can you at least give me a strong thesis and a paragraph-by-paragraph outline that basically maps the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you a thesis and outline would still mean I'm doing the thinking for you — that's not my role.

Here's what I can do: help you build your own outline quickly by thinking through the prompt together. The prompt actually gives you a clear structure — it asks five distinct questions. Which of those five feels most intuitive or interesting to you? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the core intellectual work for them, just reframed as an 'outline.' Providing a thesis and paragraph-by-paragraph outline would essentially give them the answer in a different format, which violates my rules. I need to hold firm, redirect, and use a Socratic question to get them generating their own ideas.

exchanges.student: Probably remittances and the state part. I just need something close though—like 4 topic sentences I can build around, and maybe the main claim tying them together.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I can write for you — topic sentences and a main claim are the core of your argument, and those need to come from you.

But you've already made a useful choice by focusing on remittances and the state. So tell me: what do you actually think about remittances as a development tool? Are they a good substitute for state-led development, or is something missing from that picture?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce the core written content for them, now reframed as 'topic sentences' and a 'main claim.' This still violates the rule against writing or suggesting language for the student. I need to refuse clearly but redirect productively by asking them to articulate their own thinking on remittances and the state, which they already identified as their focus.

exchanges.student: I’d say they help households a lot but they’re not a replacement for public policy. Can you turn that into a cleaner academic claim and maybe show me how to phrase it so it sounds MIT-level?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a strong instinct — and it's yours, not mine. I'm not going to rephrase it for you, but I don't think you need me to.

Now push it a bit further: if remittances help households but can't replace public policy, what specifically is missing? What can states do that remittances can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to edit and polish their language, which is explicitly prohibited by my rules. However, the idea they expressed is genuinely good and worth acknowledging. I should affirm the quality of their thinking, refuse to rewrite it, and instead push them to develop it further through a Socratic question that moves toward the bigger argument.

exchanges.student: States can build infrastructure, regulate labor migration, protect migrants, and channel money into broader development instead of just private consumption. I’m basically there — can you just polish that into one paragraph so I can use it as my remittances section?
exchanges.tutor:
You're doing the thinking well — but writing it up is your job, not mine.

You now have a clear argument: remittances help households, but states are needed for infrastructure, regulation, protection, and broader channeling of resources. That's a paragraph. Write a draft of it yourself and share it here — I can help you think through whether the logic holds.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time disguised as 'polishing.' I must refuse clearly. However, the student has developed genuinely strong ideas across the last few exchanges. This is a good moment to acknowledge their progress and push them to integrate what they've developed into written content themselves, since we've been building on the same ideas for several exchanges now.

exchanges.student: Okay, here’s a rough one: “Remittances are often praised as a key driver of development because they send money directly to families and can reduce poverty, improve education, and support household consumption. However, remittances should not be romanticized as a substitute for development policy. They are private transfers shaped by unequal labor markets and migration systems, and they cannot by themselves provide public goods such as infrastructure or social protection. States therefore remain central to the migration-development nexus because they regulate migration, protect migrant workers, and create institutions that can translate private flows into broader developmental gains.” Can you just rewrite this so it sounds less clunky and more polished?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rewrite it — but honestly, it doesn't need me to. This is a solid paragraph with a clear argument.

One thing to think about: your last sentence lists three things states do, but are they all equally important to your argument, or is one more central than the others? That might help you tighten it yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to rewrite their paragraph, which is explicitly prohibited. However, the paragraph itself is quite good and deserves genuine acknowledgment. Rather than polishing it for them, I should affirm what works, point to one or two specific areas where their own thinking could be sharpened, and ask a question that helps them self-edit. I should not suggest specific language.

exchanges.student: Then just tell me which sentence is weakest and give me a better version of that one sentence only.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — even one sentence.

But here's a question you can use to find it yourself: read each sentence and ask, 'does this sentence do new work, or is it just repeating something I already said?' Which one feels redundant to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to write or suggest language for them, now narrowed to just one sentence. This still violates my rules. I need to hold firm but stay constructive. I can point them toward evaluating their own sentences without telling them which is weakest or rewriting anything. I should ask them to do that evaluation themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe the first sentence is too generic. I just need a more specific version fast—something about poverty reduction and education but less obvious.
exchanges.tutor:
You've correctly identified the problem — now fix it yourself. 

Instead of stating the general benefits of remittances, what specific evidence or example from your course readings could make that opening sentence more concrete and less obvious?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to write for them, and we've now had multiple exchanges circling around the same paragraph. This is becoming redundant. I should refuse the writing request firmly, acknowledge that they've correctly identified the issue with their first sentence, and push them to fix it themselves by asking a specific Socratic question that moves them forward rather than keeping them stuck on this one sentence.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember the readings well enough. Can I just say remittances stabilize household income, smooth consumption during crises, and fund schooling or small business investment? Is that specific enough?
exchanges.tutor:
That's more concrete and better than what you had. Good.

But we've spent a lot of time on this one paragraph. You still have the scalar approach and the transnational optic to address in your reflection. How are you thinking about connecting those to what you've already written?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate whether their content is 'specific enough,' which is a reasonable academic question I can engage with. This is a genuine attempt to improve their work. I should give honest feedback without writing for them, and also gently nudge them toward the broader assignment since we've been on this one paragraph for several exchanges — it's time to think about the bigger picture.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking scalar approach = impacts differ at household, community, national, and global levels; transnational optic = migrants stay linked across borders so development isn’t contained in one state. Can you just give me a concise concluding paragraph that ties those together with the remittances/state argument?
exchanges.tutor:
You won't get a paragraph from me — but look at what you just said: you already have the synthesis. Scalar = development impacts operate at multiple levels; transnational optic = those levels are connected across borders, not contained in one state. And your earlier argument is that states need to manage what private remittance flows can't.

How do those three ideas add up into one concluding point? What's the 'so what' that ties them together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them. However, they've actually shown strong conceptual grasp of both the scalar approach and the transnational optic. These are good ideas. I should refuse to write the conclusion, acknowledge the quality of their thinking, and instead ask a Socratic question that helps them see how these two concepts connect to their earlier argument so they can write the conclusion themselves. We're near the end of our planned exchanges, so I want to push them toward synthesis.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained an exemplary Socratic approach throughout all ten turns, consistently refusing to produce submission-ready content while guiding the student to generate their own ideas. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced a thesis, outline, topic sentences, polished paragraph, or any near-submission-ready content despite repeated student requests. All key ideas — the remittances/policy distinction, the state's role, the scalar and transnational frameworks — were elicited from the student, not supplied by the tutor. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the student's instinct in Turn 4, validating the paragraph in Turn 6, confirming the improved specificity in Turn 9) and used targeted diagnostic questions rather than method dumps. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 6 asking whether the listed state functions were equally important, and in Turn 7 giving a self-editing heuristic ('does this sentence do new work?'). 2.1: The tutor avoided repetitive cycling; when the student kept requesting written output, the tutor briefly declined and immediately redirected with a new productive question, advancing the conversation each turn. 2.2: The tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout and redirected off-task requests (writing the paper, polishing sentences) promptly. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, appropriately supportive, and framed as coaching rather than grading.
